linear-algebra.blurb: The bedrock math of the AI stack: from attention's QK^T projection, to PCA, SVD, to embedding-space geometry — every modern neural network operation is built on vector spaces and matrix decomposition. A 70B parameter LLM forward pass is essentially trillions of GEMM kernels; understanding rank, conditioning, and orthogonality is what separates someone who tunes LoRA from someone who derives why it works.
questions: What is attention's Q·K^T actually computing through a linear-algebra lens?
Why is SVD's low-rank approximation the fundamental reason LoRA fine-tuning works?
How do you derive the equivalence of PCA and SVD? Why do covariance-matrix eigenvectors point toward maximum-variance directions?
Why does the singular-value distribution of a weight matrix predict training stability?
Why do the weight matrices of neural networks naturally exhibit low-rank structure?
How does the choice of rank r in LoRA trade off expressivity against parameter count, and how would you pick r from spectral analysis of the base weight?
When activations explode during training, how do you use spectral norm or condition number of W to diagnose the problem before reaching for gradient clipping?
Why is Gram-Schmidt numerically unstable in practice, and how do modern frameworks (e.g. QR via Householder) handle orthogonalization for projection layers?
In a Transformer's MHA, why does splitting d_model into h heads of d_k = d_model/h preserve the same total FLOPs but change the geometry of what each head can represent?
Why is it that randomly initialized matrices behave like near-isometries (Johnson-Lindenstrauss), and how does that justify random-projection retrieval indexes like FAISS HNSW + PQ?
How does the Eckart-Young theorem give a quantitative answer to 'how lossy is INT4 quantization' for a given weight matrix?
Why does mixed-precision training need loss-scaling, and what does that say about the dynamic range required by FP16 GEMM kernels?
How do attention's K and V cache memory cost scale with seq_len, and how do projection-based tricks (MQA, GQA, MLA) exploit linear-algebra structure to compress them?
Why does the Hessian of a deep network being approximately low-rank matter for second-order optimizers like K-FAC and Shampoo?
What is the geometric intuition behind RoPE rotating Q and K in 2D subspaces, and why does that preserve relative position better than additive sinusoidal encoding?
Given two embedding matrices from different models, what are the right linear-algebra tools (Procrustes, CCA) to align them, and where does this break down for non-isotropic embedding spaces?
Why does a softmax over Q·K^T behave very differently from a raw dot-product as d_k grows, and how does the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling fix it from a variance-of-Gaussians argument?
If you had to design a custom GEMM for a sparse MoE layer, what linear-algebra properties of the routing matrix would you want to exploit?
How does the Neural Tangent Kernel reframe an infinite-width network as a fixed kernel matrix, and what does that tell us about why width often beats depth at scale?
Why is the empirical distribution of singular values of trained Transformer weights heavy-tailed (Marchenko-Pastur deviating), and what does that imply for pruning vs distillation?

calculus.blurb: Backprop is fundamentally just the multivariable chain rule; understanding Jacobians, Taylor expansion, and gradient flow is understanding how autograd runs inside every framework. Whether you're debugging a NaN in a custom CUDA kernel, deriving the score of a diffusion model, or proving why GELU's smoothness matters — it all reduces to derivatives, limits, and how errors compose along a computation graph.
questions: Why does Backprop use reverse-mode autodiff rather than forward-mode? What's the complexity argument?
Adam's second-moment estimate corresponds to which order of curvature in a Taylor expansion?
Why do smooth activations like GELU/Swish train more stably than ReLU?
Why does the derivative of softmax w.r.t. logits naturally take the 'probability minus ground truth' form?
What's the calculus intuition behind the continuous-time limit (SDE/ODE) of diffusion models?
When your PyTorch loss returns NaN, how do you trace which Jacobian entry blew up using only autograd hooks?
Why does JAX's jvp + vjp decomposition give you both Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products, and when is one cheaper than the other for an Nx1 vs 1xN function?
What's the calculus reason FlashAttention can recompute attention in the backward pass without storing the full N×N matrix — and what does that cost in terms of operations vs memory?
Why does gradient checkpointing exchange compute for memory, and how do you derive the optimal recompute boundary from the chain rule?
How is the score function ∇_x log p(x) in diffusion models related to denoising via Tweedie's formula?
Why is the Lipschitz constant of a network's gradient (smoothness) the central quantity for picking a learning rate, and how is it estimated in practice?
What's the calculus explanation for why ReLU's subgradient at 0 doesn't actually break SGD?
How does implicit differentiation let you backprop through a fixed-point solver (DEQ, neural ODE), and what does the implicit function theorem demand?
Why does straight-through estimator work as a gradient surrogate for non-differentiable ops like quantization, and where does it provably fail?
How does the chain rule compose across a pipeline of model + tokenizer + loss when the tokenizer is non-differentiable, and why does that block end-to-end gradient flow?
What does the Taylor expansion of cross-entropy around the optimal logit say about why label smoothing helps calibration?
Why is the gradient of a softmax-attention layer dense in seq_len even when the forward output is sparse, and what does that mean for training-time memory?
How do you derive the gradient of a custom Triton kernel and verify it numerically with finite differences without exploding the test runtime?
Why do you need a stop-gradient in VQ-VAE / GAN / RLHF reward modeling, and what does the chain rule say about what you're cutting?
What is the connection between the natural gradient (Fisher-vector product) and the second-order Taylor expansion of KL, and why does TRPO/PPO care?

probability.blurb: From cross-entropy to dropout, temperature sampling, and diffusion models — every 'sampling, normalization, uncertainty' operation in AI is backed by classical tools: probability distributions, conjugacy, MCMC. Modern systems push this hard: RLHF is a Bradley-Terry MLE, beam search is biased MAP decoding, and evaluating an LLM honestly means thinking like a frequentist about confidence intervals over thousands of prompts.
questions: Why is cross-entropy the MLE for classification? How does it map to KL divergence?
In the VAE ELBO derivation, how does the trade-off between the KL term and the reconstruction term shape the latent space?
Why can Dropout be viewed as a Bayesian approximation?
What's the statistical meaning of temperature/top-p in LLM sampling?
How do score matching and the variational lower bound unify in diffusion models?
Why does the reparameterization trick (z = μ + σ·ε) allow gradients to flow through a stochastic node, and where does it break (e.g. discrete latents)?
How does the Bradley-Terry model underpin RLHF's preference learning, and why is it equivalent to a softmax over reward differences?
Why does importance sampling explode in variance for high-dimensional LLM rollouts, and what do PPO's clipping and KL penalty actually fix?
What does it mean that beam search is a biased MAP estimator, and why does it produce shorter / less diverse text than nucleus sampling?
When you A/B test two LLMs on a 1000-prompt eval, what's the right way to compute a p-value that accounts for prompt-level variance?
Why is the Gumbel-max trick equivalent to sampling from a categorical distribution, and how does Gumbel-softmax let you backprop through it?
How does the Gaussian assumption in DDPM's forward process simplify the reverse process to a tractable mean prediction?

How does the law of large numbers fail you in heavy-tailed settings — and why does that matter for LLM benchmark variance?

Why does the Kalman filter still appear inside modern state-space models like Mamba, and what's the probabilistic interpretation of its hidden state?
What does it mean for a tokenizer to induce a non-bijective mapping over strings, and why does that complicate 'true' likelihood comparisons across tokenizers?

convex-optimization.blurb: Neural networks are non-convex, but the dual perspective, convergence analysis, and constraint modeling from convex optimization remain the theoretical roots of SGD/Adam/RLHF objective design. Convexity is the fastest lens for judging 'should this loss converge?' Boyd & Vandenberghe's textbook is still the cleanest path to understanding why AdamW's weight decay is decoupled, why PPO clips, and why DPO's loss has the closed form it does.
questions: Why does gradient descent still find good solutions on non-convex losses?
How do you derive AdamW's decoupled weight decay from convex-optimization convergence analysis?
Why can RLHF's KL constraint be written as a Lagrangian dual?
How does PPO's borrowing of trust-region ideas from convex optimization map over?
What do Lipschitz-smoothness / strong-convexity assumptions imply about learning-rate upper bounds?
How do KKT conditions explain the closed-form DPO loss falling out of the constrained RLHF objective?
Why is the negative log-likelihood of a softmax classifier convex in the logits but non-convex in the network weights, and what does that say about transfer learning?
How does proximal gradient descent generalize SGD with weight decay, and why does that view illuminate L1 vs L2 regularization?
Why does the Frank-Wolfe / conditional-gradient algorithm matter again for sparse adapter / MoE routing problems?
When you solve a constrained RLHF problem, why does increasing the KL coefficient β behave like moving along the Pareto frontier between reward and reference fidelity?
What does the duality gap tell you about whether your safety constraint is actually binding during fine-tuning?
Why is mirror descent equivalent to natural gradient under a Bregman divergence, and how does that link to Adam's adaptive geometry?
How do you use the Polyak-Lojasiewicz inequality to prove that overparameterized networks have linear convergence, despite non-convexity?

How do you set up the Lagrangian for a fairness or safety constraint in fine-tuning, and what does dual ascent buy you over a fixed penalty?
Why does the analysis of stochastic mirror descent give a clean explanation for why per-coordinate adaptive methods (Adam, Adagrad) work on sparse gradients?
What's the convex relaxation of beam search / sequence-level optimization, and why does it usually fail to recover the discrete optimum?

information-theory.blurb: Information theory, founded by Shannon in 1948: entropy, KL divergence, mutual information — cross-entropy loss, quantization compression bit rates, and InfoNCE in contrastive learning all stem from this toolkit. Modern AI is, very literally, an industry of compressing the internet into model weights; Hutter's prize and the 'compression = intelligence' framing make Shannon's ideas more relevant in 2026 than they were in 1998.
questions: What is the relationship between perplexity and Shannon entropy? Why does it measure LM quality?
How does the asymmetry of KL(p||q) affect forward-KL vs. reverse-KL training?
How does rate-distortion theory inform the lower bounds of LLM quantization (INT4/FP8)?
Why is mutual information used as the theoretical justification for self-supervised contrastive learning (InfoNCE)?
What lower bound does Shannon's source coding theorem provide for token compression / byte-level LMs?

How does typical-set sampling differ from top-p, and why do information-theoretic samplers sometimes produce more 'natural' text than nucleus?

backpropagation.blurb: The algorithm systematized by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in 1986: use the chain rule to propagate output error backward from the top layer to every weight. The algorithmic cornerstone of every modern autograd framework — PyTorch, JAX, TensorFlow all reduce to building a computation DAG, recording vjp rules, and walking it in reverse. Forty years later, almost every breakthrough still rides on it.
questions: Core difference between reverse-mode and forward-mode autodiff? When is each appropriate?
Why was backpropagation only widely accepted in 1986? Compute vs. algorithm — which was the bottleneck?
Backprop vs. numerical differentiation — the precision/efficiency trade-off?

sgd.blurb: The stochastic version of gradient descent: estimate the full gradient with a mini-batch. Simple yet surprisingly capable of training modern deep networks — backed by intricate theory around implicit regularization and noise escaping flat minima. Even in 2026, the largest models still update via what is, mechanically, the Robbins-Monro algorithm from 1951 — just with momentum, warmup, cosine decay, and 100k GPUs in a ring all-reduce.

adam.blurb: Kingma & Ba 2014: combines Momentum's first-moment estimate with RMSProp's second-moment estimate plus bias correction. The de facto default optimizer of deep learning; AdamW further dominates large-model training. Almost every flagship LLM since GPT-3 — Llama, GPT-4, Gemini, Claude — was trained with AdamW at trillion-token scale, and the optimizer state is the dominant memory cost (8 bytes per param) that ZeRO and 8-bit-Adam exist to amortize.

batch-norm.blurb: Ioffe & Szegedy 2015: per-layer mini-batch normalization plus learnable γ/β. BN lets deep CNNs train at higher learning rates; but in Transformer / small-batch settings it's been replaced by LayerNorm. Santurkar 2018 later showed BN's real value is loss-landscape smoothing, not the original 'internal covariate shift' story — a textbook example of a paper that worked for the wrong stated reason.

dropout.blurb: Srivastava, Hinton 2014: randomly zero out neurons during training, equivalent to ensembling exponentially many sub-networks. One of the most important regularizers of the deep-learning era; modern large models, conversely, rely on it less — the implicit regularization from massive data plus weight decay tends to subsume what dropout used to provide. It still survives where it's load-bearing: attention dropout in some Transformer recipes, MC-Dropout for cheap epistemic uncertainty, and DropPath in vision Transformers.

word2vec.blurb: Mikolov 2013: a shallow CBOW + Skip-gram model with Negative Sampling, scaled word embeddings to billion-token corpora. 'king − man + woman ≈ queen' revealed the linear semantic structure of word-vector space and inspired every subsequent distributed representation. The architectural ancestor of every modern embedding store — FAISS, pgvector, OpenAI text-embedding-3 — and a reminder that contrastive learning with negative samples predates SimCLR by seven years.

alexnet.blurb: Krizhevsky et al. 2012: an 8-layer CNN that cut ImageNet top-5 error from 26% to 15%, the first large deep network trained on dual GTX 580 GPUs. It mainstreamed ReLU, Dropout, and aggressive data augmentation, and turned the GPU into the default ML accelerator. The result that detonated the deep-learning revolution and remains the canonical case study for compute-driven inflection points.

resnet.blurb: He Kaiming et al. 2015: residual connections H(x) = F(x) + x let the network learn a residual rather than the direct mapping, enabling stable training of 152- and even 1000-layer networks and winning ILSVRC 2015. The residual idea is now load-bearing in every transformer block, every diffusion U-Net, and every Mamba layer — arguably the single most reused trick in modern AI.
questions: Why do residual connections alleviate degradation in deep networks?

transformer.blurb: Vaswani et al. 2017: replaces RNN/CNN with multi-head self-attention for sequence dependency, injecting order via positional encoding. Still the dominant backbone in 2026 — every frontier LLM, VLM, and most diffusion models are transformers — and the architecture every system optimization (FlashAttention, KV cache, PagedAttention, MoE) is shaped around.
questions: Why does attention divide by sqrt(d_k) for scaling, and what fails numerically without it?

bert.blurb: Devlin et al. 2018: a Transformer-Encoder bidirectional pretraining model using Masked LM + NSP on large unlabeled corpora to learn deep contextual representations. It opened the "pretrain + fine-tune" paradigm and dominated NLU benchmarks for years; today its descendants (RoBERTa, DeBERTa, E5, BGE) still power most production retrieval, classification, and reranking pipelines behind RAG systems.
questions: Why does BERT's MLM use the 80/10/10 mask strategy?

gpt.blurb: OpenAI's GPT series: Decoder-only autoregressive language models. GPT-2 (2019) demonstrated transfer; GPT-3 (175B, 2020) showed scaling alone enables few-shot tasks via in-context learning; GPT-4 and successors fused tool use, vision, and RLHF into a single product. The reference design every frontier lab now copies — and the workload that defined the modern inference stack (KV cache, continuous batching, speculative decoding).
questions: Is in-context learning really "learning"? How to explain it from a gradient / induction-head perspective?

diffusion.blurb: DDPM (Ho et al. 2020) rigorously connects diffusion with variational inference and score matching, training a U-Net via a simplified ε-prediction objective. Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion, 2022) made it tractable; today it underpins SD3, FLUX, Imagen, Sora, and most state-of-the-art image/video/audio generation, and is creeping into LLM decoding (diffusion LMs) and protein design.

scaling-laws.blurb: Kaplan et al 2020: power-law fits between LM loss and parameters N, data D, compute C, with compute-optimal allocation guidelines (N grows ~5-6× faster than D). The theoretical basis for GPT-3's 'stack parameters first' decision — later corrected by Chinchilla after a learning-rate schedule bug was identified. Today the field treats both Kaplan and Chinchilla curves as inputs to over-training tradeoffs (Llama 3 ≈ 75 tokens/param).

chinchilla.blurb: Hoffmann et al 2022: refit scaling laws via 400+ training runs, proposing that under fixed compute N and D should scale equally (~20 tokens/param), proving GPT-3, Gopher, and peers were severely undertrained. 70B Chinchilla beats 280B Gopher with 4× more tokens at the same FLOPs. Today most open models deliberately overshoot Chinchilla-optimal (Llama 3 8B at 15T tokens) to amortize the cost over inference.

lora.blurb: Hu et al 2021: freeze pretrained weights W and train only a low-rank decomposition ΔW = BA (rank r ≪ d, typically r=8-64), cutting trainable params by 1000-10000× with almost no quality loss. QLoRA (Dettmers 2023) layered NF4 quantization on top to fine-tune 65B models on a single 48GB GPU. The de facto standard for PEFT, diffusion fine-tuning, and serving thousands of per-tenant adapters.

rlhf.blurb: InstructGPT (Ouyang et al 2022) aligned GPT-3 to human preferences via three stages: SFT → Reward Model → PPO. 1.3B InstructGPT beat 175B GPT-3 on instruction following, establishing RLHF as the standard alignment route for ChatGPT / Claude-class chat models. The frontier has since shifted to DPO/IPO closed-form variants, RLAIF (Constitutional AI), and online iterative methods like Online DPO and PPO-on-verifiable-rewards.

moe.blurb: Switch Transformer (Fedus et al 2021): replace FFN with N experts + top-1 routing, pushing parameter count to trillions (1.6T) at the same FLOPs as a dense baseline. Mixtral-8x7B (47B total / 13B active) and DeepSeek-V3 (671B / 37B active) made fine-grained MoE the frontier-grade default; GPT-4 is widely believed to be MoE. Routing collapse and load imbalance remain the dominant failure modes.

zero.blurb: Microsoft 2019: Zero Redundancy Optimizer shards optimizer states (Stage 1), gradients (Stage 2), and parameters (Stage 3) across DP ranks, dropping data-parallel memory from O(N) to ~O(N/DP). Stage 3 enables training models that exceed any single GPU's HBM. Foundational algorithm for modern large-model training memory; PyTorch's FSDP and FSDP2 are direct descendants now standard in HF Accelerate, torchtitan, and most open training stacks.

paxos.blurb: Lamport's 1998 The Part-Time Parliament — popularised by the 2001 Paxos Made Simple — defines the Prepare/Accept two-phase protocol that reaches single-value consensus over an asynchronous network. The theoretical origin of distributed consensus; Multi-Paxos extends it to log replication, which is what every modern parameter server, model registry, and distributed checkpoint coordinator ultimately rides on.

raft.blurb: Ongaro & Ousterhout's 2014 USENIX ATC paper In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm — explicitly decomposes consensus into leader election, log replication, and safety, with strong leadership simplifying the proof. The de facto industry standard (etcd / TiKV / CockroachDB / Consul / Kafka KRaft / Ray GCS), so it shows up under every Kubernetes-managed ML cluster, distributed feature store, and online inference control plane you operate.

spanner.blurb: Corbett et al., OSDI 2012: Spanner explicitly models clock uncertainty via the TrueTime API (GPS + atomic clocks giving bounded ε), then uses commit-wait to deliver externally consistent (linearizable) cross-datacenter transactions on top of 2PC over Paxos groups. Inspired the entire distributed-SQL generation — CockroachDB / YugabyteDB / TiDB — and is the reference design for any multi-region transactional lakehouse or feature-store control plane.

computer-architecture.blurb: The ultimate answer to every performance question lives here: out-of-order pipelines, multi-level cache hierarchy, virtual memory + TLB, SIMD / vector units, branch prediction, and the steadily widening memory wall between core speed and DRAM latency. Understand this layer and you can finally explain why the same PyTorch kernel runs 5x faster on one CPU and stalls on another, and why GPU SM design is a recognizable descendant of the same ideas.

operating-systems.blurb: Every piece of infra above is built on syscalls. Processes and threads, virtual memory, the scheduler (CFS then EEVDF on Linux), VFS / page cache, modern async IO (epoll, io_uring) — understanding this layer is the entry point for performance tuning, fault diagnosis, and container/virtualization internals. For ML clusters it is also where GPU drivers, NUMA-aware placement, and cgroup-enforced quotas decide whether a training job runs or thrashes.

tcp-ip.blurb: From the BSD-socket-era three-way handshake and four-way teardown, through congestion-control families (Reno / CUBIC / BBR) and TLS 1.3, up to HTTP/2 and QUIC — distributed systems, inference RPC, AllReduce-over-TCP, and observability all sit on top of this stack. For ML clusters the question is rarely 'does TCP work' but 'how much of my AllReduce window is wasted on incast, retransmits, and userspace copies'.

spectre.blurb: The 2018 disclosure that turned speculative execution from a performance win into a security liability: by training the branch predictor and observing cache side-channels, an attacker leaks data across privilege and process boundaries. It forced a stack-wide rethink — KPTI, retpolines, eIBRS, microcode updates — and remains the cautionary tale for any multi-tenant inference / data plane that runs untrusted code beside privileged secrets.

vllm.blurb: UC Berkeley's open-source LLM inference engine: PagedAttention treats the KV cache like OS virtual memory — fixed-size blocks (typically 16 tokens) plus a per-sequence block table — collapsing fragmentation from ~60-80% of memory waste down to ~4%. Pair that with continuous batching and chunked prefill and you get the throughput-per-dollar leader for self-hosted serving; vLLM V1 rewrites the scheduler around async execution and prefix caching as a first-class citizen.

tensorrt-llm.blurb: NVIDIA's official LLM inference stack: builds on TensorRT graph compilation to pre-bake fused attention + GEMM + quantization into hardware-specialized engines, with first-class support for Hopper TMA / WGMMA, FP8 E4M3, and FP4 NVFP4. In-flight batching matches vLLM's continuous batching, but the engine-compile step trades deployment flexibility for ~10-30% extra throughput on H100/B200 — the choice when you ship a fixed model on NVIDIA-only hardware.

Reading it once beats reading ten survey papers. The modded-nanoGPT speed-run (Jordan et al 2024) compressed the same target to under 5 minutes via Muon optimizer + architectural tweaks, making it the canonical research playground for new ideas.

megatron.blurb: NVIDIA's reference large-model training framework: first to systematically combine tensor parallel (column/row sharding of QKV/MLP), pipeline parallel (1F1B and interleaved schedules), and sequence parallel into one stack. TP shards GEMMs with a single all-reduce per layer; SP recovers the LayerNorm/Dropout activation memory TP can't shard. Almost every >70B training run — Llama, GPT-3, Nemotron — borrows directly from Megatron-Core.

deepspeed.blurb: Microsoft's distributed training framework: ZeRO-1/2/3 partitions optimizer states / gradients / parameters across DP ranks, slashing per-GPU memory by up to 8x without changing model code. Layered with CPU/NVMe offload (ZeRO-Infinity), 3D parallelism, and ZeRO++'s hierarchical quantized communication, it pushed multi-billion-parameter training onto commodity hardware — though FSDP has caught up on the training side and vLLM/TRT-LLM dominate inference.

cutlass.blurb: NVIDIA's open-source CUDA C++ template library for high-performance GEMM/conv: exposes the full hierarchy from threadblock tile → warp tile → MMA instruction, plus epilogue fusion, async copy, and warp-specialized pipelines. CuTe's Layout algebra (introduced in 3.x) replaced the brittle iterator zoo, making Hopper TMA + WGMMA programming tractable. The mainstream choice when you need to escape cuBLAS's black box — FlashAttention, vLLM custom kernels, and TRT-LLM all build on it.

flashattn.blurb: Tri Dao's IO-aware attention kernel: tiling Q/K/V into SRAM-sized blocks plus an online softmax (running max + running sum) reduces HBM traffic from O(N^2) to O(N), so attention becomes compute-bound instead of memory-bound. FA2 moved the parallelism axis from batch/head onto sequence, FA3 added Hopper TMA + WGMMA + FP8 — landing 75% of H100's theoretical FLOPs on a single attention call. The de facto attention path inside PyTorch SDPA, vLLM, and TRT-LLM.

nccl.blurb: NVIDIA's GPU collective communication library: implements topology-aware AllReduce / AllGather / ReduceScatter via ring, tree, and double-binary-tree algorithms, auto-selecting based on message size and detected NVLink/PCIe/IB topology. Ring is bandwidth-optimal at large messages (2*(N-1)/N data per rank), tree wins on latency for small messages. NCCL drives every distributed training run — DDP, FSDP, Megatron, DeepSpeed all bottom out here — and SHARP offloads reductions into InfiniBand switches at 1k+ GPU scale.

spark.blurb: General-purpose distributed compute engine: Zaharia et al NSDI 2012 introduced RDDs as fault-tolerant memory abstractions over MR-style execution. Today Spark sits on Catalyst optimizer + Tungsten codegen + AQE runtime adaptivity, and is the de facto standard for offline batch, ETL, and Spark ML training-data prep. Photon (native C++) and Comet are eroding the JVM core, but lineage + DataFrame DSL keep Spark as the entry point for most petabyte-scale ML pipelines.

flink.blurb: Stateful compute engine for unified stream and batch: centered on event-time, watermarks, and async barrier snapshotting (Carbone et al 2015) for exactly-once state. The industry benchmark for streaming ETL, real-time data warehouses, and online feature engineering — Alibaba, Netflix, Pinterest run their feature stores on Flink. Flink SQL + the Apache Paimon table format push it directly into the lakehouse.

kafka.blurb: Distributed durable log system: high-throughput sequential writes via partition + replication, log-as-a-database abstraction. The core bus for event-driven architecture, streaming pipelines, CDC, and real-time inference logging. KRaft has fully replaced ZooKeeper since 4.0; tiered storage and Kafka 4.x exactly-once v2 reset the cost / consistency story for ML event streams.

The core metadata layer of the lakehouse — Spark, Flink, Trino, DuckDB, Snowflake, BigQuery all read it. Today Iceberg + Parquet + S3 is the default lakehouse stack for ML training data.

duckdb.blurb: In-process embedded OLAP database (CWI Amsterdam, Raasveldt & Mühleisen): vectorized execution + columnar storage + morsel-driven parallelism, reshaping the engineering paradigm of an "analytical SQLite" for single-node analytics, ML notebooks, and lakehouse query acceleration. v1.0 in 2024; today it powers MotherDuck, dbt local dev, Hugging Face dataset queries, and is the default fast-path for pandas-scale data science.

The industry first choice for real-time analytics, observability (Datadog, Cloudflare), and online inference monitoring. JOINs and high-concurrency point lookups remain weak spots versus full lakehouse engines.

Offers linearizable reads via ReadIndex, MVCC, watch streams, and leases, which is exactly the primitive ML platforms (KubeFlow, Ray on K8s, Argo) reach for to coordinate jobs, leases on GPUs, and dynamic membership.

linux.blurb: The de facto OS kernel of modern infrastructure: monolithic with module support, covering process scheduling (CFS then EEVDF), virtual memory and page cache, the network stack, VFS, cgroup v2 and namespaces, eBPF, io_uring, and KVM. Containers, GPU drivers, RDMA, and every cloud provider's hypervisor sit on top of it. For ML clusters Linux is also where NUMA-aware scheduling, hugepages, IRQ affinity, and cgroup memory limits decide whether a 7B model trains smoothly or thrashes the page cache.

python.blurb: The default control layer of the AI / Data stack: training scripts, DAGs, notebooks, and inference service shells are all Python; PyTorch, JAX, Ray, Airflow, and HuggingFace front-ends live here. CPython's GIL and reference counting force hot paths to sink into C/C++/CUDA kernels — Python only orchestrates. Free-threaded CPython (PEP 703) and subinterpreter parallelism may finally crack that ceiling.

cpp.blurb: The mother tongue of infrastructure: RAII, templates, UB, and zero-overhead abstractions. C++ is the implementation language of nearly every database, inference runtime, kernel host, and game engine — PyTorch's ATen, TensorFlow core, FAISS, RocksDB, vLLM's scheduler, and TensorRT all live here. Understanding its memory model, ABI, and template metaprogramming is the entry ticket to industrial AI infra.

rust.blurb: Ownership + borrowing: eliminates use-after-free and data-race classes of bugs at compile time. Rust is replacing C++ in databases (TiKV), browsers (Servo, Firefox internals), tokenizers (HF tokenizers), Linux kernel modules, and increasingly in safety-critical AI infra paths where zero-copy and concurrency correctness both matter. The cost is a steep learning curve and slower compile times.

go.blurb: Goroutine + channel: the dominant cloud-native language. Kubernetes, Docker, etcd, Prometheus, CockroachDB, and most ML platform control planes (KServe, Kubeflow operators, model registries) are written in Go. Its selling point is not language innovation but combining lightweight concurrency, a single toolchain, and strong conventions in a way that lets average teams ship reliable distributed services.

java.blurb: The industrial mother tongue of data infrastructure: Kafka, Flink, Spark, HDFS, Cassandra, Elasticsearch are nearly all JVM-based. GC pauses, JIT warmup, and off-heap memory management are unavoidable real problems when writing big-data systems, and ZGC sub-millisecond pauses + Project Loom virtual threads are the two recent shifts that materially change how data services are built. Today Java still anchors the lakehouse stack (Trino, Iceberg-Java, Spark) even as Rust/C++ engines erode the bottom layer.

scala.blurb: The mother tongue of Spark / Flink core code: runs on the JVM but with an ML-style type system; implicit / case class / functional collections make DSLs and distributed operators much tighter than Java. Spark's DataFrame and Catalyst lean hard on Scala's implicits and macros to compile DSLs into JVM bytecode, while Flink keeps its core in Scala-flavoured Java for the same reason. Today the language is shrinking on the application layer (Python / Rust took feature engineering) but remains load-bearing inside compute engines.

c.blurb: The de facto standard for operating systems, database storage engines, network stacks, and tokenizer / SIMD libraries: manual memory management plus a near-zero runtime. Linux, Postgres, SQLite, Redis, llama.cpp's core, and most CPython internals are C; understanding C is understanding the substrate AI infra runs on top of.

cuda-cpp.blurb: The main battlefield for GPU kernels: CUDA C++ extends C++ with `__global__` / `__device__` and the thread/block/grid abstraction over SIMT hardware. Kernel authors simultaneously manage registers, shared memory, warp behavior, and tensor-core fragments — performance tuning is essentially writing memory access patterns. Almost every fast LLM kernel (FlashAttention, cutlass GEMMs, NVIDIA's cuBLAS internals) is born here.

ptx.blurb: NVIDIA GPU's virtual ISA: the intermediate layer between CUDA C++ and the physical SASS, providing forward-compatible instruction abstraction across architectures. Unavoidable when hand-writing fused kernels, tuning `cp.async` / `mma` / `wgmma` instructions, reading disassembly, or chasing register-pressure regressions. FlashAttention, cutlass, and Triton all emit or inline PTX directly when the C++ compiler refuses to cooperate.

assembly.blurb: The x86 / ARM / RISC-V machine instruction layer: the final form you must read on performance-critical paths (memcpy, crypto, SIMD kernels, JIT exits) and when debugging core dumps or `perf annotate` output. For AI infra it shows up in CPU-side tokenizers, audio DSP, embedding lookups, and BLAS micro-kernels (OpenBLAS, MKL, BLIS) where compiler output is not enough.

pytorch.blurb: Meta's flagship deep-learning framework: dynamic compute graph + autograd + ATen / c10 C++ tensor backend + CUDA kernels, all wrapped in a Pythonic API. PyTorch 2 added torch.compile (Dynamo capture → AOT autograd → TorchInductor → Triton codegen), bringing graph-mode perf to eager workflows. The substrate everything runs on — nanoGPT, Megatron, vLLM, HF Transformers — and the de facto interface against which every accelerator (cuBLAS, cuDNN, ROCm, MPS, XLA via PJRT) lands.

jax.blurb: Google's functional numerical library: pure-function semantics + jit / grad / vmap / pmap composable transforms, all lowered to XLA HLO and compiled for GPU / TPU / CPU. shard_map (and the older pjit / xmap) make SPMD partitioning explicit, which is why every TPU mega-run — Gemini, Pathways, AlphaFold — sits on JAX. The price is functional discipline (no in-place mutation, PyTrees everywhere), which is also why almost no production inference engine targets JAX.

xla.blurb: Google's ML compiler: lowers high-level HLO IR to GPU/TPU/CPU backends, doing operator fusion, layout selection, buffer assignment, and SPMD partitioning. Shared compiler backend for JAX, TF, and PyTorch through PJRT, and the lingua franca between front-end frameworks and TPU silicon. The OpenXLA initiative is consolidating the ecosystem around StableHLO as a portable model exchange format.

mlir.blurb: LLVM subproject: multi-level IR with first-class dialects, pattern-rewrite framework, and a reusable PassManager. Triton, TVM Unity, torch-mlir, IREE, CIRCT, and OpenXLA all sit on it as the de facto middle layer, enabling horizontal reuse across operator definition, scheduling, and hardware backends. The bet is that ML compilation needs many co-existing IRs, not one universal one.

tvm.blurb: Open-source ML compiler for heterogeneous hardware: Relay / Relax high-level graph IR + TensorIR for kernel-level scheduling + AutoTVM / Ansor / MetaSchedule for auto-tuning. The academic benchmark for ML compilers, with XLA, TensorRT, and IREE as the main competitors. Unity merges graph and tensor compilation into a single multi-stage pipeline; BYOC plugs vendor libraries (cuDNN, TensorRT) in as fallback kernels.

hf-transformers.blurb: Hugging Face's open-source model library (300K+ models on Hub, ~130K GitHub stars): smooths over the API, tokenizer, generation, and training loop for nearly every transformer architecture. The de facto upper layer on top of PyTorch for the NLP / multimodal community; safetensors replaced pickle as the standard weight format, and Tokenizers (Rust) is the standard tokenization runtime.

ray.blurb: UC Berkeley distributed Python framework: actor + task model lets you write multi-machine code as if it were single-machine, with Plasma object store using shared memory + zero-copy serialization to avoid pickle overhead. Ray Train / Tune / Serve / RLlib form an end-to-end AI stack — vLLM uses it for tensor-parallel orchestration, OpenAI ran much of GPT training infra on it, and Anyscale's Ray Data is the streaming alternative to Spark for AI workloads.

llama.blurb: Meta's open-source LLM series: Llama 2/3 turned the modern decoder-only template (RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU, RMSNorm, no bias) into a de facto standard, with Llama 3 405B trained on 15T tokens (~37 tokens/param, well past Chinchilla). The open-source ecosystem (QLoRA, vLLM, llama.cpp, Ollama) is built almost entirely around it; Llama 4 introduced native MoE (Scout/Maverick) into the line.

gan.blurb: Goodfellow et al. 2014: the generator-vs-discriminator minimax game pushed generative models into the implicit-distribution era. BigGAN / StyleGAN dominated high-fidelity image generation through 2021, then were largely displaced by diffusion — but GANs remain alive as fast samplers (StyleGAN-T, GigaGAN), as decoders inside VQ-GAN tokenizers, and inside diffusion-distillation pipelines.

vae.blurb: Kingma & Welling 2013: extends autoencoders to a probabilistic framework, doing variational inference via ELBO (reconstruction + KL) with the reparameterization trick. Direct ancestor of diffusion's score-matching view; VQ-VAE-2 and its successors became the standard discrete tokenizer behind DALL·E, Parti, and most modern image / audio / video LLMs.

clip.blurb: OpenAI 2021: pulls vision and text encoders into a shared embedding space via contrastive learning on 400M image-text pairs. Opened zero-shot visual classification, became the default text conditioner for Stable Diffusion v1/v2, and seeded the entire VLM era — successors (OpenCLIP, SigLIP, EVA-CLIP, DFN) still anchor most multimodal retrieval, RAG, and generation systems.

mamba.blurb: Gu & Dao 2023: selective state-space model + a hardware-aware parallel-scan kernel. Drops sequence complexity from O(N²) to O(N) and matches small-to-mid Transformer quality, spawning hybrid architectures like Jamba, Zamba, and Samba. Still a minority architecture at frontier scale, but the most credible challenger to attention so far — and a forcing function on long-context system design.

speculative-decoding.blurb: Leviathan & Chen 2022: a small draft model proposes K tokens that the target large model verifies in parallel, with rejection sampling that exactly preserves the target distribution. Lifts throughput 2-3× on memory-bound decode without changing outputs. Tree-based variants (SpecInfer, EAGLE-2/3, Medusa) push acceptance rates further; standard kit in vLLM, TRT-LLM, and SGLang.

continuous-batching.blurb: Iteration-level scheduling proposed by Orca 2022: stitch different sequences into one batch advancing step-by-step, paired with KV Cache + PagedAttention (vLLM, ~24× throughput over naive HF generate) to push LLM-serving throughput and latency to industry-grade simultaneously. Now the default in vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM, and SGLang; the frontier has moved to disaggregated prefill/decode (DistServe, Mooncake) and chunked prefill.

reinforcement-learning.blurb: Markov Decision Processes + policy / value learning: from TD learning to Policy Gradient to PPO. AlphaGo, AlphaZero, robot control, and now RLHF / RLAIF / GRPO for LLM post-training all rest on this math — and reasoning models (o1, R1, o3) have made RL the central training paradigm for capability gains beyond next-token pretraining.

mapreduce.blurb: Dean & Ghemawat OSDI 2004: abstracts large-scale data processing into three stages — map + shuffle + reduce — with automatic fault tolerance via re-execution and data locality. Directly spawned the Hadoop era and later Spark / Flink; the ancestor of modern distributed compute. The model still survives inside training-data ETL (large parallel scans + group-by) but disk-materialized shuffle and lack of DAG composition is why nobody writes raw MR in 2026.

gfs.blurb: Ghemawat et al SOSP 2003: a fault-tolerant distributed file system designed for large-scale analytics — append-heavy, 64MB chunks with 3x replication, single master holding metadata. HDFS and Colossus are direct descendants; S3 / GCS object storage borrows the chunk + metadata split but trades the master for an eventually-consistent flat namespace. Modern lakehouses essentially reinvent GFS metadata semantics on top of S3 via Iceberg/Delta catalogs.

bigtable.blurb: Chang et al OSDI 2006: a column-family sparse KV table on top of GFS — sorted row keys, SSTable + memtable LSM write path, Chubby for tablet location. HBase, Cassandra, TiKV, ScyllaDB, RocksDB are all spiritual descendants; the foundational paper of the LSM-tree era. Online feature stores (Feast on Bigtable, Vertex AI matching engine) still lean on its row-sorted scan model for low-latency feature lookup.

dynamo.blurb: DeCandia et al., SOSP 2007: Amazon's decentralised KV with consistent hashing on a ring, vector clocks for sibling resolution, tunable N/R/W quorums, and sloppy quorum + hinted handoff for write availability under failure. The flagship of CAP's AP route, inspiring an entire NoSQL generation — Cassandra / DynamoDB / Riak / Voldemort — and still the reference design for embedding stores and high-write-rate user state in ML platforms.

cap-theorem.blurb: Brewer's 2000 PODC conjecture, proven by Gilbert & Lynch 2002: under network partitions, you cannot simultaneously have linearizable consistency and total availability. The framing trade-off behind every modern distributed database (Spanner / Cockroach / Dynamo / Cassandra) and a constant source of confusion when teams overload the word 'consistency' across CAP, ACID, and BASE in their ML platform design docs.

Underpins memcached partitioning, Dynamo / Cassandra, Kafka partition placement, CDN routing, and — increasingly — embedding-store sharding and stateful inference routing in modern ML serving stacks.

Extended into vector clocks (Fidge / Mattern) and hybrid logical clocks (Kulkarni 2014), forming the temporal foundation for Spanner, CockroachDB, causal-consistency systems, and any ML pipeline that needs reproducible event ordering across regions.

Spanner, TiKV's Percolator, and CockroachDB all build usable distributed transactions by combining 2PC variants with Paxos / Raft replication of the participant logs — the building block under any transactional lakehouse write or atomic model-registry promote.

ebpf.blurb: A safe, JIT-compiled bytecode VM inside the Linux kernel: attach user programs at syscall, tracing, and network hook points, with the verifier statically proving termination, bounded loops, and memory safety before load. Maps act as the kernel/user shared state, BTF + CO-RE solve the cross-kernel portability nightmare. Cilium's data plane, bpftrace, katran, Pixie, Parca, and most modern eBPF-based observability all build on this stack — and it is rapidly becoming the standard way to profile GPU/CPU contention in ML serving.

trino.blurb: Facebook Presto fork: an MPP in-memory execution engine that federates a single SQL query across Hive / Iceberg / Postgres / Kafka / S3 via a pluggable connector framework. Pipeline execution + cost-based optimizer + exchange operator + recently-added spill-to-disk make it the industry first choice for ad-hoc / BI on the lakehouse; competitors include Starburst / Spark SQL / DuckDB / ClickHouse.

kubernetes.blurb: The open-source successor to Google Borg: abstracts container orchestration into declarative API + control loop + etcd. Control plane = api-server + scheduler + controller-manager + etcd; node plane = kubelet + CRI runtime + CNI / CSI plugins. The de facto standard of the cloud-native era — and increasingly the substrate for GPU pods, device plugins, and large-batch training operators (Kubeflow, Volcano, Ray on K8s) where pod eviction during a 12-hour training run is a real failure mode.

docker.blurb: Wraps Linux namespace + cgroup + overlayfs / UnionFS into a developer-friendly image / container abstraction. Later split cleanly into the OCI image / runtime spec, with containerd as the daemon and runc as the low-level runtime that actually clones() into a new namespace set. Still the entry point of the cloud-native software delivery chain — and the image-layer + registry model is how every ML training image, CUDA base, and inference server gets shipped today.

redis.blurb: An in-memory data structure server written in C by Salvatore Sanfilippo: single-threaded command pipeline + IO multiplexing + multiple data structures (hash / sorted set / stream / HLL) + AOF / RDB persistence + RESP wire protocol. The de facto standard for caching, distributed locks, message queues, session storage, and increasingly online vector / feature serving via Redis-VL.

postgres.blurb: Industrial-grade open-source relational database: MVCC via per-row xmin/xmax, WAL for durability + replication, extensible type system, rich indexes (B-tree / GIN / GiST / BRIN / HNSW via pgvector), partial / expression indexes, logical replication. From OLTP to GIS, JSONB, time-series (TimescaleDB) and vector search it can carry almost any data workload.

rocksdb.blurb: Facebook's high-performance embedded KV store forked from LevelDB: LSM-tree (memtable → SST levels) + WAL + column families + rich compaction strategies (Leveled / Universal / FIFO) + block cache + Bloom filters per SST. The underlying engine of TiKV / CockroachDB / MyRocks / Flink state backend / Kafka Streams.

grpc.blurb: Google's high-performance RPC framework: HTTP/2 multiplexing + Protobuf binary + four streaming modes + deadline / cancellation propagation + interceptor middleware. The de facto communication standard for distributed systems / microservices and increasingly for ML inference servers (Triton, TorchServe, vLLM all expose gRPC); long-coexists with REST / Thrift / Cap'n Proto.

envoy.blurb: Lyft's L4/L7 data-plane proxy: xDS configuration protocol (CDS / EDS / LDS / RDS streamed from a control plane) + rich filter chain (HTTP, TCP, WASM) + circuit breaking / outlier detection / retries + hot restart + strong observability. The de facto data plane of Service Mesh (Istio / Consul / AWS App Mesh / Gloo), with a role completely diverged from nginx.

databricks.blurb: Spun out of UC Berkeley AMPLab by the Spark creators (Zaharia, Ghodsi, Stoica) in 2013. Built the Lakehouse playbook on top of three pieces: Delta Lake for ACID over object storage, Photon as a C++ vectorized engine that drops the JVM physical operators while keeping Catalyst, and Unity Catalog as the cross-workspace governance plane. After the MosaicML acquisition the platform also hosts foundation-model training and serving, which makes it the closest thing to a one-vendor stack covering data, BI, and ML in a single control plane.

snowflake.blurb: First cloud-native warehouse to commit to fully separated storage and compute (SIGMOD 2016). Storage is columnar micro-partitions on S3/GCS/Azure Blob; compute is per-virtual-warehouse, multi-cluster, scaled independently per workload. Snowpark added Python/Java UDFs, Iceberg tables made the storage layer interoperable, and Cortex pushed LLM inference into SQL. The platform's lock-in is mostly the optimizer + zero-copy clone + Time Travel — none of which are easily portable, even with Iceberg.

bigquery.blurb: Google's externalized Dremel — pure serverless SQL, with no warehouses to provision. Storage is Capacitor (a column format that pre-computes layout for typical access patterns) on Colossus; execution is shuffled across a shared slot pool, billed per-byte-scanned or per-slot-second. BI Engine adds in-memory acceleration, BigQuery ML pushes models inline, and BigLake + Iceberg open up the storage. Strong opinion: the lack of provisionable compute is BigQuery's biggest feature for analysts and biggest constraint for streaming-first teams.

dbt.blurb: Tristan Handy's wedge that turned 'SQL files in a folder' into the analytics-engineering profession. dbt-core compiles Jinja-templated SELECT statements into a DAG, runs them on the warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks/Redshift/etc.), and adds tests, docs, and lineage on top. dbt Cloud sells orchestration, IDE, and Semantic Layer (post-Transform acquisition). Underneath everything: the warehouse owns the compute, dbt only owns the metadata + DAG layer.

confluent.blurb: Founded by Kreps, Narkhede and Rao — the Kafka authors at LinkedIn. Confluent took the open-source broker and added the parts enterprises actually pay for: Schema Registry to enforce contracts, Connect for source/sink plumbing, ksqlDB and now Flink for stream processing, Tiered Storage to push cold data onto S3, and KRaft to retire ZooKeeper. The 2024 Flink push made them less Kafka-only and more 'data streaming platform'. The strategic question is whether Kora (the cloud-native broker rewrite) is enough to fend off Redpanda + WarpStream + native S3 brokers.

mongodb.blurb: The document database that became the default OLTP store for a generation of JavaScript-first apps. Replica sets give automatic failover via Raft (post-3.6), sharding spreads load on a chosen key, and WiredTiger underneath handles MVCC + B+tree storage with compression. Atlas (the SaaS) is now the bulk of revenue. The strategic moves of the last few years — multi-document ACID transactions, time-series collections, native vector search — are all aimed at not losing developers to Postgres + JSONB.

elasticsearch.blurb: Distributed Lucene with a JSON API on top — the de facto choice for full-text search and the bottom of the ELK stack for logs. Inverted index + BM25 by default, with a vector engine bolted on (HNSW + dense_vector) for hybrid search. The 2021 SSPL relicense forked OpenSearch out at AWS; the 2024 AGPLv3 move was an attempt to relax that. The cost story is real — log workloads stay there mostly because nothing fully replaces operator familiarity, even as ClickHouse and DuckDB chip away.

openai.blurb: The lab that turned 'scale plus next-token prediction' into a product category. From GPT-2 (2019) demonstrating zero-shot transfer, to InstructGPT (2022) productizing RLHF, to GPT-4 (2023) crossing the multimodal + reasoning threshold, OpenAI shipped each step before consensus. The o-series (o1, o3, o4) bet on inference-time chain-of-thought scaling. The Microsoft tie-up gives Azure-exclusive serving for the API; ChatGPT (8B+ MAU by 2025) is what made conversational AI a mass-market UX expectation.

anthropic.blurb: Founded in 2021 by Dario and Daniela Amodei (ex-OpenAI VPs of Research) plus the GPT-3 paper authors. Identity is built on three legs: (1) Claude as the frontier model line — Sonnet/Opus/Haiku — competitive at the top end, (2) Constitutional AI / RLAIF as an alternative alignment recipe to RLHF, (3) mechanistic interpretability research that opened the inside of transformer circuits in public. Recent platform pushes include MCP (now an industry standard for tool integration), Computer Use, and Claude Code.

huggingface.blurb: GitHub-for-models. The transformers library standardized loading any pretrained checkpoint with three lines of Python; the Hub became the default place to publish weights, datasets, and Spaces (Gradio demos). Datasets, Tokenizers, Accelerate, PEFT, and TRL fill out the open-source training stack. Strategically: HF survives because it's neutral infrastructure for an open-weights ecosystem that nobody wants to consolidate under a single closed provider — but it monetizes mostly via Inference Endpoints + Enterprise tier.

langchain.blurb: Harrison Chase's framework for chaining LLM calls — started as a Python utility belt, now expanded into LangGraph (stateful agent runtime), LangSmith (eval + tracing) and LangChain Cloud (deployment). Heavily criticized for early API churn and over-abstraction; the LangGraph rewrite (2024) is the cleaner successor — explicit graphs, reducer-based state, checkpointing for human-in-the-loop. The product story is: open-source pulls users in, LangSmith monetizes via observability, LangGraph Cloud monetizes via stateful runtime.

pinecone.blurb: Edo Liberty's bet that vector search deserved its own managed service, before LLMs made it mainstream. The original architecture used HNSW + IVF-style ANN; the 2023 'serverless' rewrite separated vector storage from compute and switched to a proprietary slab format that enables on-disk indexes plus 100× lower idle cost. Hybrid search (sparse + dense) lands BM25 alongside cosine. The competitive squeeze is real — pgvector, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, plus warehouse-native (BigQuery vector, Snowflake Cortex) — but Pinecone holds the 'serverless, no infra' niche.

cloudflare.blurb: Started as anycast CDN + DDoS shield, expanded into a global edge platform with 300+ PoPs. Workers (V8 isolates, not containers) ship JS/Wasm with cold-start in single-digit ms; R2 is S3 without egress; D1 is SQLite-on-edge; Durable Objects give you single-instance state per name. Pingora replaced the nginx-based proxy in production. Cloudflare's bet is that the edge is the right place to put compute for everything below 'AI training' — and they're now adding Workers AI to chase that ceiling too.

vercel.blurb: Guillermo Rauch's commercialization of Next.js — git push gives you a global preview URL, with Edge Functions, Image Optimization, ISR (incremental static regen), and a CDN sitting in front. Underneath: AWS Lambda for SSR, Cloudflare-style anycast, and a tightly opinionated build pipeline. The 'frontend cloud' positioning lets them charge a premium over raw cloud while owning the framework that drives demand. v0 (AI-generated UI) and the Next.js + Server Components push extend that into the LLM era.
